raw_recent_text: "Ugh, not again," you spout as the old wagon shutters, then dies.
Stranded on the road again, but this time, your money has gone dry.
All but your 50 dollar bill that Grandma gave you on her last Christmas.
You've been saving it for when you needed it most, but surely, it won't be enough.
The door creaks open, and you step out into the night sky, coughing up the smoke from your fried vehicle.
After pushing your car off the road and between the trees, there isn't much else left for you to do,
so you begin to wander down the dark, lonely street.
But at the end of the road, where concrete turned to stone turned to dirt, you notice a light up ahead, on the top of a hill.
As you waltz into the old, wooden shack, your eyes begin to light up with the fire of a thousand suns.
Roulette wheels! Poker tables! And in a dark corner of the abandoned casino, sits a dealer, shuffling cards for a new round of Blackjack.
That 50 dollars might just come in handy after all. Thanks, Grandma!
As you go to sit down at the table, you hear the Dealer cough, then watch as he sits up.
In a deep, and yet strained voice, the Dealer, cloaked in darkness, poses a question to you.
Would you like to play a game of Blackjack?
You have $50

Since then, you've accumulated $20.

You're basically living paycheck to paycheck. Except there's no paycheck.
Keep pushing the boundaries, even if it's just by a little every day.
As my doctor was putting me under, he whispered into my ear:
"Life is short. Smile while you still have teeth."
Press a key to continue: ~ ~ ~ Morning, Day 2 ~ ~ ~  True
You slept so hard you forgot where you were for a second. That's the good kind of confusion.
Through your car window, you spot a rat watching you from a storm drain. It seems... curious.
Most rats run away. This one stays, twitching its whiskers.
You don't have any food to spare. The rat loses interest and scurries away.
You can't sleep. Your brain won't stop calculating odds.
If I bet X and win Y, then I could... no wait, that's not right...
You stare at the ceiling until dawn. Thinking. Always thinking. The math never adds up to freedom.
As the sun begins to set, and the stars light up in the night sky, you walk to the casino, eager to play more Blackjack.
Night falls. The game begins.
You have $70

"I'm not your fucking therapist, stop using me for emotional advice."

The sun hits your face through the windshield and you don't even mind. That's how good you feel.
Through your windshield, there's a car parked right in front of you. That wasn't there before.
Confused, and dazed, you sit up. As you open the door and get out of your car,
you notice a man, in a bright red suit, peering into your trunk.
The man sees you, and walks up to you.
"You. You're awake. Good. You know that you aren't supposed to be here? "
"This isn't a spot for people to live. This is a road for people to drive. I hope you know this."
"Do you know this?"
Press a key to continue: ~ ~ ~ Morning, Day 3 ~ ~ ~  True
The sun hits your face through the windshield and you don't even mind. That's how good you feel.
Through your windshield, there's a car parked right in front of you. That wasn't there before.
Confused, and dazed, you sit up. As you open the door and get out of your car,
you notice a man, in a bright red suit, peering into your trunk.
The man sees you, and walks up to you.
"You. You're awake. Good. You know that you aren't supposed to be here? "
"This isn't a spot for people to live. This is a road for people to drive. I hope you know this."
"Do you know this?"
"You don't know this? How don't you know this? It's super obvious stuff, man. "
"People don't live at places where they're not supposed to, and that's exactly what you're doing right now. "
"I'd suggest you stop it, right this instant."
After the man tells you this, he looks up, and stares at the sun. And after about 20 seconds, he rubs his eyes, walks back to his car, and drives off.
For once, you sleep peacefully. No dreams. No nightmares. Just rest.
You wake up feeling... good? Is this what normal people feel like?
It won't last, but you appreciate it while it does.
As the sun begins to set, and the stars light up in the night sky, you walk to the casino, eager to play more Blackjack.
Back for more? The cards appreciate your persistence.

You wake up feeling like a million bucks. Well, maybe a few thousand bucks. But still great.
A horn blares right outside your car, rattling the windows. Looking out, you see a man in a freight truck.
But something's different. He's not laughing. He's... crying?
"I'm sorry... I'm so sorry for all those times I honked at you. "
"I was going through some stuff, man. My wife left me. My dog died. My truck? Also dying."
He wipes his nose with his sleeve.
"Here. Take this. It's not much, but... I want to make things right."
The trucker hands you a wad of cash through the window, still sniffling.
Your new balance is $47 + $286 = $333
"Drive safe, friend. Drive safe."
And with that, the freight truck slowly pulls away, the horn playing a sad melody into the distance.
You wake up to scratching noises. Something is ON your car.
You look up. A family of raccoons is making themselves at home on your roof.
Mom raccoon. Dad raccoon. Three baby raccoons. All staring at you through the windshield with those little bandit masks.
You honk the horn. They don't flinch. You honk again. The babies yawn.
You stare at each other for a full minute. They're not leaving. This is their car now.
You go back to sleep. In the morning, they've left a dead mouse on your hood. A gift? A threat? Both?
As the sun begins to set, and the stars light up in the night sky, you walk to the casino, eager to play more Blackjack.
Come, sit down, we have a game to play.

If you didn't know any better, you would have guessed you were on a movie set.
Walking towards the front desk, you see a cheery old lady, who looks up from her computer to smile at you.
Her gray hair covers her glasses, and her hand trembles as she hands you a pen and a clipboard with some paperwork.
Of course it's paperwork.
After filling out your information, you walk back to the front desk, and hand the lady the clipboard. She smiles, and begins to speak to you.
I see you're here for a checkup. The Doctor will see you now.

Your sinuses are completely blocked. I can see the inflammation from here. Sinusitis. Antibiotics and decongestants.

You walk back to the front desk to checkout.

"You got the look of a man runnin' from somethin'. I seen it before. Hell, I been there myself."

"Nice pile of cash you got there," he rasps, nodding at the money in your passenger seat.
"You look like a collector. A finder of rare things."
He glances around nervously, then leans closer.
"Name's Gus. Grimy Gus, they call me. Got a little shop down on Fifth and Nowhere. "
"If you ever find yourself with... unusual items... things that don't belong in the light of day... I can make them disappear. For a fair price."
He taps his nose conspiratorially.
"Collectibles, trinkets, treasures. Things you picked up on your... adventures. I don't ask questions. Just cash on the barrel."
He hands you a grimy business card through the crack in the window. It reads: "Grimy Gus's Pawn Emporium - We Buy What Others Won't"
"Come by sometime. You won't regret it. Probably."
He shuffles off into the morning mist before you can respond.

You get in your car and drive to Tom's Trusty Trucks and Tires.
"Well howdy there, stranger! Name's Tom. Welcome to Tom's Trusty Trucks and Tires!"
The old mechanic wipes his hands on a rag that's seen better days.
His eyes are kind, but tired. The kind of tired that comes from years of hard work and harder choices.
"You got the look of a man runnin' from somethin'. I seen it before. Hell, I been there myself."
He gestures to a faded photograph on the wall. A younger Tom, standing with a woman and two small children.
"Family's a funny thing, yunno? You don't realize what you got 'til it's gone. "
"Spent years chasin' money, chasin' dreams. Almost lost everything that mattered."
He taps the photo frame with a calloused finger.
"But it ain't never too late to go back. That's what I learned. It ain't never too late to pick up the phone and say sorry."
He looks at you with something like recognition.
"Anyway. What can I do for ya today?"
As the sun begins to set, and the stars light up in the night sky, you drive over to the casino, eager to play more Blackjack.

Back again? Let's get this show on the road.

raw_recent_text: The familiar green felt stretches out before you, and across from you sits the Dealer,
shuffling cards with practiced ease.
You're dealt a hand. A King and an Ace. Blackjack.
But instead of paying out, the Dealer's face contorts with rage. He slams his fist on the table.
"You think you can just WIN?! You think it's that EASY?!"
His yelling gets louder and louder. At first he's yelling at himself, muttering about odds and luck. But then, he turns to you. His eyes bore into yours.
"This is YOUR fault. ALL OF IT."
The screaming grows deafening until you jolt awake, heart pounding.
The Dealer's rage echoes in your mind long after you wake.

Your current sanity: 32%
The sun hits your face through the windshield and you don't even mind. That's how good you feel.
You drive your car to a gas station for supplies. Just snacks. Maybe some coffee.
You're browsing the chips when the door slams open.
"EVERYBODY ON THE GROUND! NOW!"
A man with a shotgun. Ski mask. Shaking hands. This is really happening.
The cashier freezes. A mother clutches her child. An old man drops his coffee.
The robber swings the gun around, wild-eyed.
A man with a shotgun. Ski mask. Shaking hands. This is really happening.
The cashier freezes. A mother clutches her child. An old man drops his coffee.
The robber swings the gun around, wild-eyed.
You duck behind a shelf. Slowly, carefully, you crawl toward the back.
The robber is focused on the register. You're almost to the storage room...
You make it. Hide behind boxes of toilet paper and canned goods.
You can hear everything. The shouting. The crying. The crash of the register hitting the floor.
Then sirens. Running footsteps. Silence.
The police find you an hour later, still hiding. Curled up like a child.
Your thoughts are fracturing. The shadows are getting closer.
=== CAR TROUBLE ===
The temperature dropped overnight. Your door locks are frozen solid.
You can't get into your own car.
You spend an hour breathing on the lock, rubbing it with your hands.
Eventually it thaws. But you've lost precious time.
A familiar gold truck rolls up and parks behind you. Tom jumps out, wiping his hands on a rag.
"Well I'll be. Didn't I just sell you this thing? What'd you do to it?"
He circles the car, muttering to himself, then crouches down and peers underneath.
"I can patch this up right here. Won't take long. Call it "$150" for the trouble."
Repair for $150?

After wandering from your vehicle, you find yourself deep in the woods.
Squirrels run by and up into the trees. The sun hits every branch and casts a shadow below.
And you wander on a natural path, journeying into the unknown.
As you walk along the path, you find a mother deer, with two children, walking the path towards you.
As you get closer, the mother appears cautious, but then runs in your direction, before stopping before you.
Her two children follow behind, and before you know it, the three of them wait in front of you.
You put your hand out, and pet the mother deer. She makes a happy squeak noise, and wags her tail.
She touches her head to yours, then continues down the path, with her two children following.
Eventually, you get to the end of the path, and find the main road. You follow it back to your wagon, and take a seat, to rest for a moment.
As the sun begins to set, and the stars light up in the night sky, you drive over to the casino, eager to play more Blackjack.
The neon signs blur together. For a moment, you can't remember why you came here.
You've got that look in your eye. Hope. Or madness. Sometimes they're the same.
The Dealer looks at you strangely. His jade eye narrows.
He does NOT seem pleased to see you tonight.
You have $11,374

after playing a few rounds of blackjack, the dealer points to the door.
without questioning his word, and with your winnings in hand, you scurry to the door, eager to get some sleep.
you make it to your car and drive away from the casino,
and you park in a little alcove on the side of the road.
you lay down, and close your eyes. it's time to rest.
the dream takes you back to the casino. but this time, you're not at the bar.
you're sitting at a blackjack table, a drink in your hand.
the ice clinks against the glass as you take a sip.
"care to be dealt in?"
you look up at the dealer, and your blood runs cold.
the dealer looks exactly like you.
same face. same eyes. same everything. they smile at you-your own smile, but somehow wrong. twisted.
"is something the matter?"
you shake your head. "no. nothing's wrong."
you smile back and take a long sip of your drink. the other you deals the cards,
and you play in silence, the only sound being the shuffle of cards and the clink of chips.
you wake up, unsure which one of you was the real one.
the line between player and dealer feels blurrier than before.

your current sanity: 33%
the sun hits your face through the windshield and you don't even mind. that's how good you feel.
a glossy black limousine is quietly approaching your wagon.
as you sit up from your slumber, you read 'oswald's optimal outoparts' cursively engraved in gold letters on the side of the limo.
waving the vehicle down, the limo slows, then stops before you.
the door opens vertically, and a large red carpet is rolled out onto the street.
you watch in awe as a man, with a combover and a tuxedo, walks out before you. he coughs, then speaks.
"why hello there! the name's oswald, as you can see by my nametag.
do you like my bowtie? well of course you do! it appears your limousine has broken down."
oswald pulls a gold whistle out of his pocket, and blows into it deeply.
"oh stuart!" you watch as a bald man in a tailcoat suit, no taller than 4 feet, hobbles over to oswald's side.
"this is stuart! he will fix your limousine up for a fair price.
let's say, i don't know, i suppose a fair price is 500,000 dollars.
okay, the look on your face says that i'm making a big mistake.
let's try $900, and stuart here will get you back on the road! do you accept?"
"really? you don't want my services? i'm so sorry stuart, but it appears they don't want our services."
stuart begins to break down into tears, and he runs quickly back into the limo.
"shame on you! shame on you! i hope to never see the likes of you again."
you watch as oswald rolls up the red carpet, gets back in the limo, and drives off into the distance.

Like smoke that decided to sit down.
